Those elusive Revival gods... here's some lovely words about her by Ross Nichols, from his poem IDYLL:


The fire of growth that leaps within the tree
is the instant fire of spring
when Celi moves the sap, the power without and within
the sky that calls up the life.

In the buds and the blossoms Niwalen
comes, the tall daughter tree-high:
her footprints are tiny spring flowers,
in her hand is the wand of power
and the sign of the benign star.

Yellow her cold tresses float liberal
and clothe her waist-deep.
She holds by a leash the thin wind-dogs
that harry the clouds of sleep.

In the autumn throned with her children
in a bronze palace of leaves
there is her home, where the apples
are piled with the rye-sheaves.

And the fires again are burning, but dimmer
with the fallen leaf
the red leaf and the yellow, and the dry wood
of age transforms from its grief

replies.content:
One I've dropped in snippets in previous Magic Mondays. There is an animated work out of Japan by the name of Puella Magi Madoka Magica. I'd been dimly familiar with it for years, and my tolerance for jerking pictures on screens being ever so slightly higher than our host's (i.e, once in a blue moon rather than never) I decided that I should actually go ahead and watch it last year. I was more than a little surprised to find that a televised work of all things had some of the most obvious higher-plane influence I've ever seen in a work, period. (The *only* other work I've seen/read and remember that I consider definitely in the same league when it comes to obvious higher-plane backing is The Divine Comedy itself, though I'm a bit thin on experience with a few other obvious suspects like the Norse sagas and Journey to the West.) [1]

The thing is, there's a rather well-known story about that show's production that I was familiar with from back in the days before my TV tolerance really imploded. The show originally aired early in 2011, and was already shaping up into a major hit, except the production itself was a bit of a disaster internally (not exactly unheard of for the creators) and in danger of implosion... until the Tohoku earthquake and tsunami hit, which forced the airing of the last two episodes of the show to be delayed by a little over six weeks anyways (thus ensuring that the studio would have time to finish). But the real fun part? The final episode aired on Good Friday 2011 (which, yes, means that the Tohoku earthquake hit two days into Lent)... and the events of that episode are strikingly reminiscent of the (IIRC deuterocanonical) story of the Harrowing of Hell, which of course takes place between the Crucifixion on Good Friday and the Resurrection on Palm Sunday. Moreover, IIRC I've seen multiple fan analyses (definitely at least one) from people more familiar with Buddhism comparing one of the show's characters (the central character of the last episode) to Kannon Herself, and the Maria Kannon hypothesis that Mary Theotokos and Guanyin/Kannon are aspects of the same deity is one with a long history. [2]

This is the sort of thing that raises one's eyebrows even *before* you start factoring in the possibility that Maria Kannon has caused earthquakes before...

(The show and the franchise it spawned is also quietly notorious for how frequently new releases coincide with natural disasters in general. On top of the Tohoku quake, one of the recap movies (a common-ish anime thing that you don't get in US stuff) had its US release about the time that Hurricane Sandy hit, the sequel movie's Japanese release was a week before Super Typhoon Haiyan hit the Phillipines, and an animated adaptation of a game spinoff that features a virus-themed antagonist released in early 2020 and, well, yeah. I have standing quatloos on either the Japanese or US release of the announced second sequel movie occurring in 2023 and coinciding with a destructive Tropical Storm or Hurricane Idalia.)

[1] - Speaking of which, an older not-directly-related data point: I'm the commenter who had the same kind of experience that JMG had with Batman. The character I had that experience with is from this show, back when the show had caught my attention as possibly of interest but I hadn't yet concluded it was worth going ahead and actually watching something on screen.
[2] - The show's religious influences are a mix of Buddhism, Christianity, and something else entirely that I recognize instinctively but I'm pretty sure hasn't quite formed yet. (Whether that last is a new pantheon or a a changing of an old one is a matter of more than a little personal interest.)

- pretentious_username

With tarot and oracle cards, I find each deck to be significantly different. In general, reading tarot helps me to tell stories about my perceptions and feelings. I find some decks seem to volunteer and speak to family matters, others seem to speak to my personal and inner development. I prefer tarot when reading for other people. I do my best to let go of knowledge of them or their situation and just read the cards, it has proven at best informative and affecting, and at worst entertaining. Sometimes I'll explain the cards' general meanings and the person I'm reading for will immediately tell the rest of the story, and I can watch as they go deeper into the imagery and their own processing.

I've noticed you commenting about heathenry. I have the Yggdrasil Oracle and have found it very communicative. It has been particularly communicative about internal matters, my personal state, and my journey. I've also found it an aid for prayer and meditation.

comments.title: Invoking versus banishing
comments.content:
When is it appropriate to invoke, and when to banish? I am thinking of both the pentagrams and the hexagrams. Are there times when it is rude to banish an entity, or - on the other hand - imprudent not to? What are the appropriate criteria to make such a determination?

In Circles of Power you’ve likened invocation to turning on a faucet or switching on the power and banishing to shutting it off. When do we need to switch on the power, and when to shut it off?

Cantankerous Crustacean

replies.title: Re: Invoking versus banishing
replies.content:
Okay, let's start with the basics. Ceremonial magic -- which is what we're discussing -- is always done in a particular sequence. You establish a magically active space, you invoke some specific energy into it, you do something with that energy, you banish any excess energy so it doesn't just hang around, and then you close the magically active space. That's the framework for every operation more significant than a simple daily practice.

That right there is the answer to your question. You only invoke when you're going to do something specific with the energy -- for example, consecrate a talisman, talk to a spirit, empower yourself through a working for spiritual development, enter into the elemental realm in meditation or scrying, or what have you -- and when you're done, you banish. The two aren't done separately. Even when you're in the learning curve, it's best to follow that sequence -- perform the ritual to open a temple, do an invocation of an elemental or planetary force, sit down and meditate on the force, then banish it and perform the ritual to close the temple.

Does that help clarify things a bit?

replies.title: Re: Invoking versus banishing
replies.content: Thanks! That’s the way I’ve always done any ceremony other than daily practice, as per your instructions in that book, so I’ll stick with it.

replies.title: Re: Invoking versus banishing
replies.content: If I may follow up, how does one use the opening/closing ceremonies? For example, to work with an element, does one always do the appropriate opening for that element and then a pentagram ritual to invoke the element? Or are there cases where the opening alone is sufficient without the pentagram ritual?

replies.title: Re: Invoking versus banishing
replies.content: Okay, the opening and closing ceremonies I'm discussing aren't specific to any of the elements. In the basic Golden Dawn system, you perform a Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram to clear the space, purify with water, consecrate with fire, circumambulate three times, and invoke the Lord of the Universe with the usual words ("Holy art Thou...). Then you proclaim the working you're going to do, and you follow that with whatever invocation of energies you have in mind. For one of the elements, I generally use the elemental pentagram ritual followed by the invocation of the element from the corresponding grade ritual, just because I like working with energies at high intensity, but you can do one or the other of these alone if you want to. The best thing to do is experiment, and see what results you get.

replies.title: Re: Invoking versus banishing
replies.content:
Ok. I think I've been more or less on the right track, then. For meditating on Fire, say, I would open a temple - purify with water, consecrate with fire - stopping to invoke Fire in the South before the circumambulation and invoking the Divine. For a working other than meditation, I would add the pentagram ritual to that to up the intensity. Sounds like I could even go another level higher in each case. I'll just try it different ways and see what happens.

Thank you!

replies.content:
Here is an article discussing the pros/cons of "speed reading" as a technique, bringing in both the research and the personal experience of the author (who is a pretty impressive self-teacher):
https://www.scotthyoung.com/blog/2015/01/19/speed-reading-redo/

The short version: speed reading (eliminating sub-vocalization, looking at fewer spots per line, moving on whether you feel sure you "got it" or not) has some limited usefulness, but falls short of some of the more extravagant claims of its more enthusiastic teachers.

My own experience is that speed reading produces a very shallow kind of comprehension and is more effective for absorbing facts than for engaging critical thinking/evaluating what I read as I read it. It also comes to a screeching halt on words you don't have in your immediate-access vocabulary (I taught myself speed reading for a History of Anglo-Saxon England class, and I frequently tripped up on exotic Old English names).

I've had some success using speed reading as one piece of "Inspectional Reading" as spelled out by Adler and Van Doren in How to Read a Book. "Inspectional reading" or "intelligent skimming" is when you read to get as much as you can in a limited amount of time. It's helpful for figuring out if you want to read a book more carefully, and as a first step for a more careful reading of a book. It's also plenty adequate for a lot of lighter-content books like business, self-help, and popular psychology books.

That being said, over the past year and some change, I've personally found it useful to counteract my natural "gotta read 'em all!" tendencies and purposefully cultivate an attitude of "it's better to read fewer books more deeply", and so I've been doing less with skimming and speed reading and more with taking notes and writing summaries and such.

Your mileage will almost certainly vary, of course, but hopefully some of this is helpful!

In various ways I've been drawn to the nordic pantheon and then had that attraction confirmed through dreams, divination, synchronicities and other means. At first learning about heathenism was very balancing for me. I had a sacrifice myself before hurting another mindset that didn't mesh with being a parent and making my way in an occasionally hostile society (particularly as we made culturally-controversial life path decisions.)

As I learned more though, I found in reading the Eddas that I didn't want to read my kids the heathen stories (with lots of fighting, theft, rape, etc.) anymore than I wanted to read them the Bible stories (with many of the same themes.) I also became wary and hesitant about actively worshipping the primary gods, particularly Odin. I recall something I read here at some point; that all Gods have traditions associated with them, not only what they give but what they expect. So, with Odin, yes he may be wise and grant passage to Valhalla, but he also asks that you die bravely, actively fighting and seeking glory for your family/people. I worked with Thor for a while, and sought him particularly for protection of family, and for an increase in my own bravery. So, this isn't a vote of "no, don't go there," but more of a proceed with caution and be very clear about what you ask for and what you offer in return. I guess that goes for all dieties though.

As I've studied more of the lore of not only the Norse, but also the Anglo-saxons, I've found other ways to honor this connection I have with this pantheon while avoiding the big names. As I understand it, the Aesir as we know them were Gods of the kings and the priests, one of the reasons their lore is so developed and well preserved. There are countless other dieties that were worshiped by the common people and known to be milder, like the Vanir gods Frey and Freya. I've enjoyed going deeper into the lore and actually found more enduring connections with minor dieties, such as Foresti (another god of justice,) Skirnir and Gna (diplomat and messenger,) Nerthus (goddess of counsel,) and Mokk (defeater of nightmares.) There isn't much written about any of them, just enough to get an idea, but the threads connected to those ideas seem to run deep.

replies.title: Re: The Sphere of Protection of the Cross
replies.content:
Congratulations open_space, please keep us posted when you publish the book. I will probably buy a copy.

Regarding the problem with divination. As you know, the Church does speak very ill against Astrology and the Tarot, to the point of equating those with satanic practices. Please let me share a personal experience.

In 2016 I received a Kerigmatic initiation, and part of the ritual as the re-affirmation of the commitments my parents and godparent took on my behalf during Baptism. Those oaths were extended with a few additional statements, that include both the explicit rejection of Astrology and the Tarot. I reluctantly accepted, wondering if the obvious loophole of using a different divination system was acceptable or not. The moment I uttered the words, "I do reject", an insight was downloaded to my mind: "Children shall not go around the kitchen and poking the boiling pots; but they shall be told everything they need to know about the food".

When I tried to make sense of it, I came to the conclusion that, as much as premarital sex, divination is one of those quirky rules Our Lord demands from their flock; divination is not evil, not even wicked, but it's under a restriction meant to train our individual wills in alignment to His Will. The second realization I came immediately after was that,- for lack of a better term,- information about the future was not banned but restricted in a way that we cannot command it on demand. The word that came with that last was *augur*.

Under that premise, carthomancy (including Tarot), geomancy, the I-Ching and other techniques that use specific actions to elict specific answers are probably not Ok. On the other hand Astrology is just reading the signs that the Lord already put up there in the sky for anyone smart enough to lift their sight and figure out what each of these stars *mean*. That is not a path I can follow, given the initiation I mentioned before, but that is not a general rule.

Other possibility is dream interpretation. We dream every night, but lack control on what we dream about (or remember afterwards). I think it would be valuable if you supplemented your system with the practice of asking the Lord "what do you want to tell me this night" as part of your nightly prayers, and then writing down what you remember of your dreams and how that correlates with what you are living at the time.

replies.title: Re: The Sphere of Protection of the Cross
replies.content:
"Sacrilege", originally from roots meaning "theft of something sacred", now refers to forbidden forms of disrespectful treatment or misuse of people, places, and things which had beforehand been properly sacralized and defined as deserving better respect. Looking this up, "things" seems to also include "Sacred Scripture"; I assume they didn't have the modern distinction between patterns of information and material objects at the time. So using passages from scripture or liturgy with a profane or non-prayer magical intention might be taken to qualify.


I looked at a few search results about the Catholic canons governing religion-related publications. This is the US Conference of Catholic Bishops' overview:

https://www.usccb.org/about/doctrine/publications/upload/permission-to-publish.pdf

This article from "Canon Law Made Easy" touches on the subject:

https://canonlawmadeeasy.com/2011/03/21/index-of-forbidden-books/


If this is the kind of project where you're trying to stay technically within the letter of the canon, you'll want to make sure that your publication either doesn't qualify as a book of prayers or catechetical material and is not intended for use as a textbook, or is not intended to exist in printed form.

More generally, you may want to be clear on exactly to what degree your devotion does and doesn't demand that you respect different explicit or tacit precepts or teachings. To make sure you have a feel for what kinds of rules you'll be straining or breaking, try to imagine what would go through the heads of the relevant authorities if you tried to get the nihil obstat and imprimatur, or if you had a spiritual director and tried to clear the project with him. Also of course make sure you are familiar at least with §2115-2117 of the most recent catechism (part 3, section 2, chapter 1, article 1), and know whether and where you disagree with the justifications it claims.

My limited experience, for what it's worth, suggests that whatever the forces are that are actually behind all this, they will go to significant lengths to find a way to work within strictures that are derived with due diligence from a sincere devotion. While this would be in one sense comforting, it would also mean that it would be hard to tell whether you guessed "right" on how heterodox/orthodox etc. to be. It's not like we can straightforwardly take partial derivatives in the average of the fruits with respect to the quantitative degree of orthodoxy and locate the optimum on a graph; but even if we could, this meeting-halfway from the other side might mean that the peak would be very wide and hard to distinguish against the inherent noise.

I tried to join other pagans. I reached to the local permaculturists and meet them, but I was very disappointed. They believe in all kind of things and yes, they do spiritual practices (mostly buddhist), however, when I talk to them I have the impression that they have void brains or drug abuse. I joined an online atheopagan group, and they look like the kind of people I'd love to meet, but alas, no one lives nearby. The only caveat is that they suffer from excessive wokism.

The garden is still progressing, even with the recent draught. The spirit of the garden has not talked to me yet, but the image of what it can become is slowly forming in my mind. Some people love what I am doing (restoration in dryland garden), the others just complain we don't grow food. It's not easy to grow food when the soil is not good, we are not allowed to irrigate and the weather has been so dry lately. Still, my priority is building up soil.

My wife and I are expecting our second child to come next March. Looks like a shy girl.

So far, the update. Next, I want to raise a problem I don't know how to handle.

My father has recently passed away (not from the unmentionable virus, it was just too much drink). He was a mystic that believed all the New Age stuff; even when he was barely able to walk, he insisted he was perfectly fine, as if by admitting he was not right, he could become ill. Our relationship was a little distant, he was more like a misbehaving elder sibling to me.
When his couple told us he was back again at the hospital, I knew this was it. I dreamed of it, and visions came, all of them were pointing the same, so it was not a surprise when his couple called us saying the doctor gave him only a few hours left of life. I think I was the last one to see him alife, sedated, he could not talk, but I think he could listen, for his breath changed upon my words. I don't believe in God, but I prayed for his soul, and my father looked relieved. He seemed to accept it calmly, and he faded.
I am grateful for the spiritual practices you taught us. I think this allowed me to feel all the pain of my father's death without losing my mind.

His death comes with a set of problems that I have to face now. He was divorced. My mother is a professional witch, she's making a life reading cards, cleaning auras and making candle rituals. Her bookshelf only has occult books for Saints, Angels, Candle Magic and such, and she has her altar inside the bedroom. I don't know if she is a good witch in the sense that her magic works, but I know she's not a good person, she's very egotistic and claims to be right even when faced with facts. There's a long story involving an appartment that belonged to both my parents: my father wanted it for me, my mother wants it not for anyone of my lineage. I would gladly sell the appartment and share the money with my mother, but there are some legal issues that we have to solve first.
What I fear from this situation is what I don't know. I'm pretty sure my mother has been doing rituals to have things going her way, and I am afraid that some of her bad will ends up hitting me. Do you know any way to protect me from her backfiring?

To make things more complicated, I have a sister I don't talk with. She's a good for nothing that has vampirized my father the last fifteen years. You can tell because my father was insanely thin while my sister is extremely overweighted. She lives now on subsidies and in another appartment that my father bought for renting, but then my sister installed her butt in the appartment and my father lost his income. She just wants to keep that appartment for herself.
The mother of my father is still alife, she is not in her senses but has some estate, which my aunt and uncle are managing. They want my sister to live happily in her not earned appartment, at my expenses, of course. They have some money to support her, if that comes to happen.
In my father's wake, they were all very kind to me. Suspiciously kind. No scolding, advances or admonitions this time. I'd say they fear I sell the appartment where my sister is living and force her to work for a living. I wouldn't do that. I have no interest in doing damage for the sake of it, and besides, that would risk my sister coming to the city where I live, and I want her living as far away as possible from me.

So far, they've all bugged me with what I have or have not to do (mother, sister and father's family). They've been calling every day for checking how we are going to pay things, what documents do we need now, talk with the renters and such. No one is caring about my father Personality. No one is caring about how I feel the losing, but my wife. I was overwhelmed and tell them all to calm down and wait until we can talk with the lawyers, in a couple of weeks.

I asked in one divination session how to balance all the above. The result was a geometric figure: three triangles that joined to form a fourth triangle. Meditating on it, I see that there's no way I can balance it if I leave one of the 'factions' outside. The only way to balance things out is to piss off them all equally. If any of the factions gets what they want, the others are going to be awfully pissed off.
Another thing that hit me is that the superposition of the triangles can be another triangle or it can be the void shape of a triangle, so what were previously thought as three full triangles are actually truncated triangles. In other words, they all think I belong to them, and what belongs to me is a part of their 'shared' possesions, but it is not. Each one of them is talking about our family possesions, but in every case, 'family' is a different set of people. What they think they have is just an illusion, so they are gonna hate it whatever I do, once the illusion vanishes.

I know you are not the kind to involve yourself with such social issues, but do you have any advice on how I can cope with the stress and stay focused?

replies.title: Re: Strange Numerology
replies.content:
1) He's going to have a lot of hard work ahead of him, with that combination of name and birth numbers, but at least they're in harmony. He's going to need to learn patience, to take care of practical details, and to accept frustrations and difficulties -- but if he does that, his capacities for achievement are immense.

2) I don't recommend any kind of intensive occult practice at that age -- his astral body has just finished becoming wholly independent (that usually happens around age 14) and so he's going to have to deal with the impact of puberty on his subtle bodies before he can start doing serious work. That said, there's a lot he can do in other ways. Learning at least one system of divination would be helpful, not least because it can give him guidance when he needs it; learning some natural magic, especially for protective purposes, would also be useful. In terms of reading material, myths and legends are excellent stuff at any age and are particularly useful to get the mind stocked with the raw material of magical symbolism. If he's interested in any religion, whether it's a local one or one from abroad, that's also a direction he can explore. And of course having someone older who will listen to him and take his concerns seriously will make an immense difference.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hey JMG, thanks for these Magic Mondays. They're quite the help on the path.

What's your take on the occult significance of the Jungian animus/anima, if any? The more I poke at the stranger aspects of reality that are discussed 'round these parts, the more convinced I am that Jung was (to really reduce it) essentially repackaging occult ideas in a way palatable for polite materialists. That's not a dig on the guy - I think his work is magnificent.

I think there's probably multiple books worth of discussion to be had on that, but I am specifically asking about the animus/anima as I haven't yet found a direct analogue in my occult studies. The concept almost feels like a mix of spirit guide and internal 'tester/challenger/tempter' but not precisely, especially with the gender aspect which seems integral to the concept, so I'm curious to hear your take, and happy to pursue any suggestions you may have for stuff to read up further about this question, if you have any!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's an exceedingly complex question, because (as with anything involving Jung) we're dealing partly with central European occultism, partly with the distinctive collective psychology of early 20th century Europe, and partly with Jung's own personal psychology. The animus/anima, the contrasexual image, is particularly loaded in this case, because Europe in Jung's time was dealing with a fantastically convoluted and dysfunctional set of attitudes about sex and gender -- not that our current set is especially healthy, mind you, but it's improved noticeably! I recommend a close read of Bram Djikstra' fine book Idols of Perversity as a way to understand just how tremendously conflicted people were in Jung's boyhood about that whole realm of human experience.

In Jung's time it was very, very common for spiritual influences to act through the anima because most men, especially in the intellectual classes, had repressed their own spiritual and intuitive capacities as "not manly." That's less true nowadays, and so the anima is being picked up and used as a vehicle by other forces in the psyche -- if you compare Jung's comments about anima possession to some of the phenomena behind the current transgender movement, well, let's just leave it there, shall we?

As I see it, all the archetypes are forms, through which various forces can act. How the anima functions for you, in your own magical work and inner life, depends on what forces happen to be constellated through that form -- and that's a personal matter. All this is by way of saying that there isn't a specific occult factor assigned to the anima, because occultism works primarily with forces, and many different forces express themselves that way -- or through each of the other archetypes.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you very much for the comprehensive reply.

I'd not considered the context of Jung's times and our times at all - to my embarrassment - which gives me a great deal of new things to ponder and a whole slew of interesting trains of thought. I'll be sure to check out Djikstra's book!

replies.title: Re: A question about chakras
replies.content: The centers of the Middle Pillar are not the chakras, full stop, end of sentence. The chakras are located along the spinal column and relate to the nerves that control the endocrine glands. The centers of the Middle Pillar are along the midline of the body, some inches forward from the spine, and don't affect the endocrine system. That's why the Golden Dawn system uses them. Western esoteric systems are safe to practice without constant supervision by a teacher, because for so many centuries teachers of occultism were in constant danger of being executed by the churches or murdered by mobs whipped up by religious fanatics; everything had to be communicable in written form, so that as long as instructions survived, the secrets wouldn't be lost. So you don't have to worry about it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Good day JMG,

What does Occult Philosophy have to say about the concept of "natural rights?" If we do indeed live in a universe that is blissfully indifferent to human concerns and aspirations, the existence of a universal moral code for humans that's antecedent to man-made legal systems/governments, seems like a rather hard sell.

The secular version of "natural rights" that's a product of post-"enlightenment" rationalism seems like a corruption of the concept Cicero and some of the Stoics originally espoused (Natural Law) and then was much later revised by Thomas Aquinas who threw his own Christian spin on it. The later rationalists further bastardized this concept and made it out to be a series of Faustian demands that the universe must grant us this and that "right"....well, because reasons.

The only modern thinker who (in my view) seems to have a sober view on this issue is Edmund Burke, who essentially takes the old Ciceronean position on Natural Law and expressed extreme wariness of conflating this Natural Law with man-made decrees, customary laws, and other temporal expediencies. Burke also seems to embrace the ternary, rejecting both extreme positions, (1) that Natural Law means any random opinion one pulls out of their backside should become a law, because it's a product of Human Reason and stuff!, (2) There is no such thing as Natural Law (and thus natural rights) because it's not something I can touch, taste, smell, hear, or see. The ideas of Rousseau and Bentham, respectively, seem to embody these extremes.

Anyway, from an Occult perspective, it seems to me that the only "rights" we have are really obligations to take our nature/god/gods/wyrd/providence/necessity-given human gifts/faculties and put them to productive use for spiritual gain, and by that on the mundane/legal level, governments have an obligation to not hinder its citizens from developing their natural gifts (within reason), and any liberties they grant citizens based on this obligation could be framed as "rights." In summary, it seems any rights that are justifiable (from an occult perspective) are dependent first on natural gifts and then duties. And any "right" without a corresponding duty is a broken equation, in my opinion. Am I off base here in terms of what occult teachings might say on this matter?

-Obsidian Tottering Raccoon

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Well, it depends on the occultist, of course. Those who follow one of the monotheist faiths tolerably often claim that their god endowed them with certain natural rights. From my polytheist Burkean perspective, by contrast, a right is an agreement among the members of a community, and that's all it is. It exists because, and only because, the members of the community agree (using some mechanism such as a public vote or a legislature) to grant that right to one another. A set of rights that has been established for a long time, and shown to be good for the achievement of human happiness and ethical greatness, deserves to be upheld even at the cost of lives -- but it's still a human construct.

In nature there are no rights and no responsibilities, just actions and consequences. When someone says "I have a natural right to X," they're trying to claim unearned power, and bully you into granting them some privilege without thinking it through first. Nature does not care if you use your gifts and faculties well, or ill, or at all -- she just sees to it that you get the consequences of your choices. That's what freedom means. You are free to be an idiot if you want to be one!

I haven't revisited since either getting into occult philosophy or rethinking Libertarianism pretty seriously, but ESR is a careful thinker and a clear writer, so you might find some further food for thought there, even if you disagree with some or much of it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thanks for this.

While I may totally disagree with their worldview, I can at least respect atheist materialists who stick to their guns and call out natural rights as being an absurdity.

This does seem to be a major division within the libertarian community. And funny enough, it was a recent debate I had with a (right-leaning) anarchist that spurned my own deeper investigation into this philosophical question. During the "debate" (it barely was one) I simply pointed out that claiming any sort of rights antecedent to community agreements or laws is an epistemologicaly-untenable position, especially when those rights are not attributed to a deity or some other supernatural phenomenon. My interlocutor seemed utterly stunned at the position I was taking and quickly resorted to leftist-type retort rhetoric, replying with something nonsensical like "Wait, so since you're denying human rights then you believe it's ok to murder innocent children?"

It seems like a lot of people these days of various ideological persuasions don't understand their own positions very well and are rather unequipped to defend the ideas they unreflectively see as being self-evident. And thus the tendency to have an emotional meltdown whenever their beliefs are called into question.

comments.title: Weekly query
comments.content:
Hi all,

I'll keep it short this week since our host will no doubt have lots of responses as usual, this being a very active space these days.

Here we go:

Have you noticed any changes to planetary hours, that is, their usefulness or significations, when the planet governing them is retrograde (or making a station)?

I have used the hours to good effect so far but I have not paid attention long enough to see if there's a pattern here with regard to retrogrades or other temporary conditions.

Thanks!

-DA777

replies.title: Re: Weekly query
replies.content: Yes, I've found that you can still use planetary hours when the planet is retrograde but you have to keep the influence of the retrogradation in mind. As I've noted here a few times now, a retrograde is fortunate for anything that begins with "re-" -- thus if I want to revise a story, for best results I can begin in the day and hour of Mercury during a retrograde.

comments.title: doing ritual magic in visualized form
comments.content: I completed Learning Ritual Magic and enjoyed doing some of the exercises in visualized form.  Now I'm working through Circles of Power.  Would it behoove me do some of those ritual in visualized form?  If so, are there precautions one should take to make sure it's done properly?  Also, why would one do magic in visualized form?  I'm assuming it helps work more directly with the etheric and/or astral realms?  Thanks.

replies.title: Re: doing ritual magic in visualized form
replies.content: It's always best to do serious magical workings on the material plane when you can, since while you're incarnate, that's where your energies are going to be strongest. That said, you can certainly do rituals now and again in visualized form, because it's a superb exercise for training your abilities of concentration and visualization. But it's a way of building magical strength, not something to do when you need maximum effect.

replies.title: Re: Tortoise and the Hair
replies.content:
1) I close it at midnight sharp, to keep my workload down. Now and again I might slip up and put something through afterwards, but please try to get it in before midnight.

2) Hair has significant effects. I grow my hair and beard long partly because I like the look, partly because I'm cheap and don't like to pay for haircuts or shaving supplies, but also because long hair has a significant effect on the brain. Your skull is transparent to red light, and your pineal gland is sensitive to light. Catholic and Buddhist monks shave their heads to maximize the light exposure of their pineal glands, because that helps put them in a state of consciousness suitable to their spirituality; Jews wear skullcaps and Taoist and Orthodox monks grow their hair long because that helps them with the state of consciousness they need, which is a different one.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Thank you as always JMG for Magic Monday!

In my times in the occult scene I've noticed a very marked tendency towards people taking themselves extremely seriously. This I note is something that John Keel writes about the UFO contactee scene in his book on Mothman: a marked humorlessness amongst the UFO enthusiasts. This tendency towards seriousness I've found salient after laughing my way through Keel's book on my fifth or sixth reread. The hijinks he describes are, indeed, absurd and hilarious, and much of what I've seen over the past 13 years or so I've spent hanging out with occultists and mages and madmen are, equally, absurd and hilarious. Yet, remarkably, I've seen relatively little sense of humor as a part of the occult culture, although certainly individual occultists are indeed uproariously funny with a very healthy sense of proportion. Reflecting on the matter, of all the subcultures I've experienced and participated in, the magical scene appears to me to probably tend towards taking itself the most seriously with the least sense of humor, at least writ large. This marked tendency towards humorlessness I've noticed as holding steady across all the parameters various identities, too, such as political affinity, ethnicity, sexuality, neurodivergence, morality, or aesthetics. Basically, the only pattern that I can discern is that the more competent occultists I know tend to have very well-developed senses of humor and proportion, but still occultists as a class seem to way more often take themselves extremely seriously than any other group I have personal experience with. Do you have an understanding, JMG, as to why occultists might so often tend to have such little sense of humor, sense of the ridiculous, or sense of the absurd?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I wish I knew. Humorlessness is a severe weakness, psychologically but also strategically; if you're closed to a sense of the absurd, you routinely fail to notice your own absurdity, and so you do absurd things that land you in various messes. Philip Carr-Gomm likes to say that Druids should cultivate three mystical senses -- these being common sense, a sense of proportion, and a sense of humor. It's good advice and should be taken very, very seriously -- ahem -- by every occultist.  ;-)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I certainly don't have the experience or depth of observations you do, but one thing came to mind reading your description: UFO Enthusiasts and Occultists are both groups of people who are used to most folks outside of the group treating their interests with scorn and ridicule. Add in the fact that if you're interested enough in UFOs or the occult to be part of a "scene", then it's likely very important to you, and it sounds like a recipe for brittle insecurity - at least if you both take yourself and the opinions of others too seriously.

comments.title: SOP with Golden Dawn Practices
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Is it possible to use a banishing SOP and then do the GD Middle Pillar? Is it bad practice to do planetary magick ie Hexagram rituals after performing the SOP?

replies.title: Re: SOP with Golden Dawn Practices
replies.content: I don't recommend it. If you want to do the Middle Pillar exercise and the Hexagram rituals, why not just use the LBRP as your basic ritual? It's a perfectly good rite and it meshes flawlessly with the rest of the GD system.

replies.title: Re: SOP with Golden Dawn Practices
replies.content:
Hi JMG,

This then begs the question, what would be the appropriate way to use planetary and zodiacal magic in conjunction with the SOP?

replies.title: Re: SOP with Golden Dawn Practices
replies.content:
Those possibilities of the system haven't been developed yet. John Gilbert wasn't much into practical magic, and meant the SoP primarily as a personal spiritual discipline and protective ritual, not a first step toward a complete system of ceremonial magic. Could it be developed in that direction? Sure, but that hasn't happened yet. If you want to do planetary and zodiacal magic, the standard GD rituals are probably a better choice.

(Another option would be Renaissance-style astrological magic, which ought to fit with the SoP without difficulty.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
"Power" is a nightmarishly vague word. It can mean quite literally dozens of things, which is why I tend to avoid it. I prefer Eliphas Lévi's framing of influence moving through the astral light in response to will. You can influence yourself or others, but your attempts to influence others will always rebound on you; thus it's usually most effective to work on yourself and let the effects radiate outward from you to the universe.

Those who will not master themselves try to master the world. That attempt usually blows up in their faces in one way or another.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: With the idea that it takes three lifetimes of intense occult practice to reach Gwynfydd, what age does that assume starting at?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The idea is that the first life goes into reorienting yourself to the spiritual side of life, and that's less a matter of practice than of experiences and shifts in attitudes. Once you've done this, your second life in the sequence will be devoted to practice -- you'll be drawn to some system of occult or religious practice in your teens or thereabouts, and spend the entire life doing the work. Then in the third life you'll also be drawn to some system of practice early on, and you'll also have the opportunity to clear away any remaining karma that has to be shed before you finish with incarnation. (This life tolerably often ends early, and sometimes rather messily.)

If you're just getting to the path in middle age, say, this may be your first life in that threefold sequence. Alternatively, depending on your karma, you may have a more roundabout route to work through; I certainly have.

2) I have an ephemeris, a printed copy of my horoscope, a spiral notebook and a pen. For casting charts, I have an old edition of SolarFire, though I've also used the Rosicrucian Fellowship software with good effect; I don't use those to track transits, though.

I would note that many prominent Whole Sign proponents acknowledge the benefit of quadrant systems like Placidus, and aim to find out how best to combine the two. comments.title: Fetus development vs evolutionary history.
comments.content:
This morning I was meditating on the doctrine of the early development of human body mirroring the evolutionary development of the human race. Several questions came to mind.

1. Not really a question but an observation. In uterus, the embryo/fetus development mirrors the development of our lineage through Animal Kingdom, from unicellular life to homo sapiens. C-section pushes almost ready but not quite fetuses into babyhood. Overpopulation pushes almost ready but not quite animal souls into humanhood. Both C-sections and overpopulation are the biological byproducts of our Faustian, technological pursuits. Does that seem correct?

2. Early childhood, IIRC, mirrors the intellectual and emotional development of the individual soul through its several incarnations. What is the cut line for when this process is complete? 3 years old? 4 years old? Certainly no more than 7, right?

3. If the individual development is essentially complete by the time the child is of school age, what is the metaphysical meaning of adolescence? Does the maturation process in the teen years trace anything like, say, karmic balance?

replies.title: Re: Fetus development vs evolutionary history.
replies.content:
1) Yes, that's broadly correct.

I'm curious about the way the world *actually* works instead of the overly-rationalistic way we're told it works.)

As a more minor sub-question, I don't appear to have genetic or other ties to the relevant part of the world for this practice; I would assume that it's fairly common for incarnations to 'move' around various parts of the world, and where you've lived and died previously doesn't necessarily determine where or who you'll be the next time around?

replies.title: Re: Question on Incarnations

In my life before this one, I was an American woman of Swedish descent; this time around, I'm a typical American genetic gazpacho of Scots, Welsh, English, several other things not yet traces, and a bit of Lakota. As far as I can tell these two bodies had no ancestors in common this side of the Bronze Age.
